entry.title: Data Integration in ABAP Cloud: Data Consumption using External Entities
entry.content:
In the previous post, we discussed data exposure using SQL services. In this post, we shift the focus to the data consumption scenario.

Imagine an ABAP Cloud system that needs to access data located elsewhere, perhaps in an SAP HANA Cloud database, or even in another ABAP system. With SQL Services, an ABAP system can itself act as a database. To support such outbound data access in a clean and cloud-compliant way, ABAP Cloud introduces a new CDS modelling artifact: CDS External Entities.

 

What Are CDS External Entities?

CDS External Entities enable outbound SQL access from an ABAP Cloud system. This means you can:

  • Access data from a remote database
  • Load and process remote data in ABAP
  • Combine remote data with local data

In ABAP Cloud, classic secondary database connections are not available. CDS External Entities are their more powerful and cloud-ready replacement.

Conceptually, a CDS External Entity is comparable to an SAP HANA virtual table; it represents a local view of a remote table or view. The remote object and its fields may have different names, which can be mapped explicitly.

CDS External Entities can be consumed in two different ways:

- Dynamic Access

In the SAP BTP ABAP environment, a CDS External Entity can be used directly in ABAP SQL. You write a SELECT statement that accesses data from a remote database, loads it, and processes it in ABAP.

- Static Access

Starting with release 2508, CDS view entities and classic CDS views can be built on top of external entities. This allows you to integrate remote data seamlessly into your CDS view modelling and treat it like local data. This feature will be used in my next blog post of the series.

Logical External Schema

Alongside CDS External Entities, there is a second important artifact: the Logical External Schema which is based on SAP HANA Smart Data Access (SDA). The key features are as follows:

  • It represents a remote system at design time.
  • It is mapped to a concrete remote source at configuration time.
  • It contains connection-related information (host, port, user, etc.).

From a runtime perspective, the ABAP system sends the query to its own HANA database, which then federates the query to the remote system using SDA.

 

How External Entities Work at Runtime

External entities allow remote data access using a new ABAP SQL syntax:

SELECT FROM <external_entity>

  PROVIDED BY <logical_external_schema>

  INTO TABLE @DATA(result).

In this flow:

  1. The ABAP application sends the SQL query to the local HANA database.
  2. HANA performs query federation using Smart Data Access.
  3. Data is retrieved from the remote database.
  4. Results are returned to the ABAP application server.

The <logical_external_schema> is a design-time artifact that points to the remote system and is bound to an actual remote source during configuration.

 

Example Scenario

Let us build an example based on the travel scenario in which

  • SAP HANA Cloud acts as the remote database.
  • The ABAP system consumes data via Dynamic CDS External Entities.

It is crucial that

  • The external entity has the same name and field structure as the remote HANA table.
  • Field names and types must match exactly (HANA is case-sensitive).

Besides, to enable the access, an outbound service and a communication scenario are required.

Design Time Steps

1. Define a Dynamic CDS External Entity

@AccessControl.authorizationCheck: #CHECK
@EndUserText.label: 'external entity for travel'
define external entity ZI_EE_TRAVEL_000 external name TRAVEL
{
  key TravelID      : abap.numc(8)                external name "TravelID";
      AgencyID      : abap.numc(6)                external name AgencyID;
      CustomerID    : abap.numc(6)                external name CustomerID;
      @Semantics.amount.currencyCode: 'CurrencyCode'
      BookingFee    : abap.curr(16,2)             external name BookingFee;
      @Semantics.amount.currencyCode: 'CurrencyCode'
      TotalPrice    : abap.curr(16,2)             external name TotalPrice;
      CurrencyCode  : abap.cuky                   external name CurrencyCode;
      BeginDate     : abap.dats                   external name BeginDate;
      EndDate       : abap.dats                   external name EndDate;
      Description   : abap.sstring(1024)          external name Description;
      OverallStatus : abap.char(1)                external name OverallStatus;
      CreatedBy     : abp_creation_user           external name CreatedBy;
      CreatedAt     : abp_creation_tstmpl         external name CreatedAt;
      LastChangedBy : abp_locinst_lastchange_user external name LastChangedBy;
}
with federated data 
provided at runtime;

This entity models the structure of the remote table or view.

2. Define a Logical External Schema

This acts as a placeholder for the remote database and is referenced by the external entity.

3. Create Outbound Service and Communication Scenario

  • Create an SQL type outbound service for the logical external schema
  • Create a communication scenario and assign the outbound service to it. Then save and publish the scenario locally.

This enables administrators to configure access to the remote system

4. Consume the External Entity in ABAP SQL

Create a class and write ABAP SQL code

Select * from my external entity provided by my remote into table my local table

CLASS zcl_travel_consume_000 DEFINITION
  PUBLIC
  FINAL
  CREATE PUBLIC .

  PUBLIC SECTION.
    INTERFACES if_oo_adt_classrun .
  PROTECTED SECTION.
  PRIVATE SECTION.
ENDCLASS.

CLASS zcl_travel_consume_000 IMPLEMENTATION.
METHOD if_oo_adt_classrun~main.
     data: travel type table of zi_ee_travel_000.

    select * from zi_ee_travel_000 provided by z_travel_remote_db_000 into table @Travel.

    out->write( travel ).
    endmethod.

ENDCLASS.

In summary, what happens at runtime is as follows

  • The ABAP system sends the query to its HANA database
  • HANA SDA forwards the query to the remote database
  • Data is fetched and returned to the ABAP application
  • The local internal table is filled with remote data

Now we need to establish the connection between our development system and the SAP HANA Cloud database.

 

Configuration Time Steps

1. Create Communication Arrangement

In the SAP Fiori Launchpad:

  • Create a communication arrangement for the communication scenario you published in design time.

2. Maintain a communication system for the remote database

  • Activate Remote SQL-Access
  • Choose HANA ODBC as the SDA adapter
  • Provide host, port, driver, and additional connection settings
    (These details are typically provided by the system administrator)

save the communication system.

3. Maintain Remote Schema Information

In the communication arrangement:

  • Specify the schema name of the remote HANA database
  • Save the configuration and check whether the connection is successful

4. Prepare the Remote HANA Database

In Visual Studio Code and using SAP HANA Database Explorer:

  • Create a table that matches the CDS External Entity exactly
  • Ensure field names, types, and sizes match as well

Remember that SAP HANA is case-sensitive.

What Happens Behind the Scenes

When you execute the ABAP SQL statement:

  • The ABAP SQL layer resolves the external entity and logical external schema
  • HANA automatically:
    • Creates a remote source
    • Creates the required virtual table
  • Query federation is handled transparently

In this sense, CDS External Entities act as a smart abstraction that automatically manages HANA remote sources and virtual tables via ABAP design-time objects.

Press F9, and the data is returned as expected.

In the next blog post I would like to build a RAP application in the cloud, based on data I read from an on-premises system. So, stay tuned!

entry.title: Now That You Have Talent Intelligence Hub, How to Maximize It for Workforce & Succession Planning
entry.content:
Implementing Talent Intelligence Hub (TIH) is a major milestone—but the real value comes from how effectively you use it. When fully enabled and adopted, TIH becomes a powerful engine for workforce planning, succession planning, and internal mobility, helping organizations move from reactive talent decisions to proactive, data-driven strategies.

Kudos to @RinkyKarthik  for the incredible work she is doing with the Talent Alchemy podcast and for the SAP SuccessFactors Talent Ecosystem!  A recent series on the show sparked a critical question: How can you maximize TIH for workforce or succession planning?  It’s a timely discussion that aligns perfectly with the insights in this blog post.

Below are key ways to maximize TIH and turn talent data into meaningful workforce insights.

 

  1. Enable and Optimize Talent Search

One of the fastest ways to unlock value from TIH is by ensuring Talent Search is enabled and properly permissioned.  Included in the Succession module, Talent Search is a powerful tool to assist in locating internal talent. 

 

  • Confirm that the right users (HR, talent partners, leaders) have access.
  • Make sure Skills are included in the search criteria under Ratings & Attributes.

Why it matters:
This allows you to quickly identify internal talent based on actual capabilities, not just job titles or resumes. For workforce planning, this means you can spot skill clusters, gaps, and ready-now talent across the organization.

 

  1. Use the Role Readiness Indicator for Successor Selection

Also within the Succession module, the ability to nominate successors to a position is fundamental for talent planning.  When selecting successors, enable the Role Readiness Indicator.

 

  • This displays a candidate’s readiness directly on the position nomination card.
  • The readiness indicator pulls from CDP (Career Development Planning) data.

Why it matters:
Leaders no longer have to guess whether someone is “ready now” or “ready later.” This creates more objective, transparent succession discussions and helps prioritize development actions for high-potential employees.

 

  1. Permission “View Role Details” in the Succession Org Chart

One final piece within the Succession module is to ensure users can access View Role Details directly from the Succession Org Chart.  The role detail view links to the job profile, also referred to as job description. 

 

  • This connects the role to the job profile from the Succession Org Chart (SOC).
  • Leaders can see role expectations, skills, and role requirements in context while actively planning when it’s needed most.

Why it matters:
Succession planning becomes role-centric, not person-centric. Leaders can align successors to what the role truly requires, improving bench strength and reducing risk when planning for critical positions.

 

  1. Turn On Suggested Roles in the Career Worksheet

The Suggested Roles functionality on the Career Worksheet enables employees to proactively seek new roles.  Suggested roles can use TIH associated tagged attributes as criteria for suggested next-step opportunities.

 

  • Recommendations are based on proximity of role in career path, associated attributes, roles in same job family, popularity of role within team, and open positions (if SF RCM is enabled).
  • Employees get clearer guidance on realistic career options.

Why it matters:
This drives employee ownership of development while aligning individual aspirations with organizational needs—creating a healthier, more sustainable succession pipeline.  From the employee perspective, they can see other roles in the organization where their skills and competencies are aligned and possibly create a new career aspiration they were unaware of.

 

  1. Enable Career Explorer

Career Explorer enables employees and leaders to visualize potential career paths across the organization and recommends opportunities based on the career paths of similar employees. This feature replaces the Suggested Roles functionality in the Career Worksheet, so it is recommended to enable one or the other—not both.

 

  • Shows adjacent, stretch, and future roles.
  • Highlights skill similarities and gaps between roles.

Why it matters:
Career Explorer supports internal mobility and future talent pipelines. Succession planners can use it to reveal non-obvious successor pools that may have transferable skills but sit outside traditional career paths.

Note:  There are specific prerequisites that are required to enable career explorer, such as Employee Central, Job Profile Builder enabled with mapped job classification codes, Succession enabled and at least 1,000 employee job change records, identified by job code changes. 

 

  1. Leverage the Opportunity Marketplace (additional license required for legacy licensing model but included in CTD new licensing)

The Opportunity Marketplace is a one stop shop for employees and managers to browse, create, enroll in and manage different types of opportunities.  Opportunities include internal projects, mentoring programs, temporary work projects and learning items. 

 

  • Employees can explore gigs, projects, and stretch opportunities to advance career options.
  • Each opportunity can be tied to skills and role requirements.

Why it matters:
Short-term opportunities accelerate skill development, making employees “succession-ready” faster. Employees who are engaged in their own development and seek out internal skill-building opportunities produces lower attrition numbers.  Leaders also gain real-time insight into who is building experience in critical skill areas.

 

  1. Drive Skill and Attribute Adoption Across the Workforce

The true power of TIH depends on participation. Create a campaign to introduce the Growth Portfolio and promote the ability to see the job architecture structure created for your organizations. 

  • Encourage managers and employees to work together to fill out and assess their skills and attributes in the Growth Portfolio.
  • Reinforce this during performance, development, and career conversations.

Why it matters:
The more complete the data:

  • The richer your succession plans become
  • The more accurate your workforce analytics are
  • The better your organization can forecast talent risks and opportunities

In short: better data = better decisions.

Final Thought: From System to Strategy

TIH is not just a system—it’s a strategy enabler. When fully enabled and widely adopted, it connects skills, roles, readiness, and opportunities in a single talent ecosystem.

By activating the features above and encouraging employee engagement, organizations can:

  • Build stronger succession pipelines
  • Identify future skills early
  • Improve internal mobility
  • Make workforce planning truly data-driven

TIH gives you the insight—how you use it determines the impact.

Implementing Talent Intelligence Hub (TIH) is a major milestone—but the real value lies in how effectively it is utilized. When fully enabled, TIH serves as a powerful engine for workforce planning, succession planning, and internal mobility, shifting organizations from reactive talent decisions to proactive, data-driven strategies.

entry.title: Identifying Leaders via Direct Reports in the Performance–Potential Matrix (Succession Planning)
entry.content:
Introduction

In one of our recent project, we had a business case where the client wanted to identify and filter employees who are leaders or managers within the Performance–Potential Matrix in Succession Planning. Additionally, they wanted the Succession icon to display only for those employees who hold a leadership role.

As per current system design in Succession Management, there is no standard field directly available for identifying leaders based on team size. To meet this requirement, we configured a simple yet robust solution using Integration Center, custom fields, standard field and matrix icon configuration.

To achieve this outcome, we used Integration Center to find whether an employee is a leader based on the number of direct reports. This logic was then associated to both a standard field (to control the Succession icon) and a custom field (to enable filtering within the Performance–Potential Matrix).

Below are the step to be followed:

1. Select any unused standard field—in our case, we used businessSegment—and rename it to “Is Leader?”. Define a picklist with the values Yes/No to this field.

2. Choose an available custom field, name it “Is Leader?”, and define the same Yes/No picklist.

3. In Integration Center, configure if/then/else conditional logic using the teamMemberSize field from the User entity for both the standard and custom field.

  • If teamMemberSize ≥ 1, populate the field with the optionId for Yes.
  • Otherwise, populate it with the optionId for No.

(Ensure the correct picklist option IDs for Yes/No are used.)

 


 

 

 

 

4. Validate both that data is populating correctly in the standard and custom fields using Test View.

5. Once the values are populating as expected, configure Scheduling so that the data continues to update regularly in both fields.

6. Download the latest Succession Data Model and update the attribute matrix-filter="yes" on the respective field. This enables business users to filter leaders/managers directly in the Performance–Potential Matrix.

7. Configure succession icon for standard field 'businessSegment'
 
8.  As a result, the custom field is now successfully added as a matrix filter in the Performance–Potential Matrix. Choose 'generate' to get output.
Conclusion: 
This solution provides a way to identify leaders using existing system capabilities. By combining Integration Center logic with custom and standard fields, organizations can enhance Succession Planning insights without requiring complex development or extensions.

Thank you for reading this article!

Feel free to share your thoughts, questions, or alternate approaches.

entry.title: BTP HANA Cloud Runtime Environment: Other or Cloud Foundry
entry.content:
Hi all,

we are receiving some mixed signals here. We are say broading our footprint on BTP. Especially we have requirements to use HANA Cloud as persistence layer on BTP. Something we did not do yet

When it comes to runtime environments we have two choices

  • Other
  • Cloud Foundry

When looking at the resources, I get mixed signals

  • Undefined
    • Within help there is (at least what I found) little evidence, when to use which.
  • Leaning towards Other
    • When looking at community, I fould this multi-part blog that seems to outline, that "Other" is an addition and improvement from a former only possible Cloud Foundry
  • Leaning towards Cloud Foundry (? but maybe I misinterpret)
    • Developing SAP HANA in the Cloud Foundry Environment | SAP Help Portal

Currently my guts feeling is, using Other Environment is way to go, but not sure I got that right. Please help 🙂

Cheers

Jens

entry.title: SAP Stammtisch (03/2026)
entry.content:
Liebe Stammtischschwestern und -brüder, der nächste Stammtisch steht an und wir freuen uns riesig, euch wiederzusehen!
Als SAP Enthusiasts leben wir vom Austausch, von guten Gesprächen und neuen Impulsen – und genau dafür suchen wir noch einen passenden Veranstaltungsort.
Wenn ihr eine Location kennt oder selbst etwas anbieten könnt, meldet euch gerne bei uns!

Bitte meldet Euch via MeetUp an, damit wir alle Anmeldungen in einem Tool sehen!

https://www.meetup.com/sap-enthusiasts-munchen/events/313381590

entry.title: Request SAP CodeJam Belgium - Joule Studio: Create Joule Skills and Agents Without Coding
entry.content:
Dear,

We, SITBE organizers, would like to host an SAP CodeJam on September 18th in Kontich, Belgium the day before the SAP Inside Track.

Proposed Dates:
We would like to combine it with the SAP Inside Track Belgium, on the 18th the CodeJam and on the 19th the SAP Inside Track.

Topic: Joule Studio: Create Joule Skills and Agents Without Coding

We would be very grateful for the opportunity and would greatly look forward to welcoming the SAP CodeJam expert.

https://community.sap.com/t5/sap-codejam-blog-posts/sap-codejam-topics/ba-p/221407#codejam1a 

Thank you in advance!

Kr, Wouter
